title: AdamW Optimizer
summary: A modification to the Adam optimizer that decouples weight decay from the gradient-based update. By applying weight decay directly to the parameters rather than through the loss function, AdamW avoids the interaction between adaptive lea…

papers.citation: 2019 · Ilya Loshchilov, Frank Hutter · Decoupled Weight Decay Regularization

sections.body:
A modification to the Adam optimizer that decouples weight decay from the gradient-based update. By applying weight decay directly to the parameters rather than through the loss function, AdamW avoids the interaction between adaptive learning rates and regularization that degrades generalization in standard Adam.

In standard SGD, L2 regularization (adding \( \frac{\lambda}{2}\|\theta\|^2 \) to the loss) and weight decay (subtracting \( \lambda\theta \) from the parameters each step) are mathematically equivalent. However, in adaptive optimizers like Adam, this equivalence breaks down. When L2 regularization is applied through the loss, the gradient of the regularization term \( \lambda\theta \) is scaled by Adam's per-parameter adaptive learning rate \( 1/(\sqrt{\hat{v}_t} + \epsilon) \). This means parameters with large historical gradients receive less regularization than those with small gradients — the opposite of what is typically intended.

AdamW fixes this by applying weight decay directly to the parameter update, after the adaptive gradient step:

\[ \theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - \eta \left( \frac{\hat{m}_t}{\sqrt{\hat{v}_t} + \epsilon} \right) - \eta \lambda \, \theta_{t-1} \]

where \( \hat{m}_t \) and \( \hat{v}_t \) are the bias-corrected first and second moment estimates, \( \eta \) is the learning rate, \( \lambda \) is the weight decay coefficient, and \( \epsilon \) is a small constant for numerical stability. The key insight is that the decay term \( \eta \lambda \, \theta_{t-1} \) is not scaled by the adaptive factor, ensuring uniform regularization across all parameters.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Standard Adam couples weight decay with the adaptive gradient scale, while AdamW decouples it, applying decay directly to the weights.

AdamW is the default optimizer for training Transformers and most large language models. It pairs particularly well with learning rate warm-up and cosine annealing schedules. Typical values are \( \lambda \in [0.01, 0.1] \) and \( \eta \) tuned via warm-up.

title: SwiGLU Activation Function
summary: A variant of the Gated Linear Unit (GLU) that uses the Swish activation as its gating function. SwiGLU replaces the standard ReLU-based feed-forward network in modern Transformer architectures (LLaMA, PaLM) due to consistently superior e…

papers.citation: 2020 · Noam Shazeer · GLU Variants Improve Transformer

sections.body:
A variant of the Gated Linear Unit (GLU) that uses the Swish activation as its gating function. SwiGLU replaces the standard ReLU-based feed-forward network in modern Transformer architectures (LLaMA, PaLM) due to consistently superior empirical performance across language modeling benchmarks.

The general GLU family applies an element-wise gating mechanism in the feed-forward sub-layer. A standard GLU uses a sigmoid gate: \( \text{GLU}(x, W, V, b, c) = \sigma(xW + b) \otimes (xV + c) \). SwiGLU replaces the sigmoid with the Swish function, defined as:

\[ \text{Swish}_\beta(x) = x \cdot \sigma(\beta x) \]

where \( \sigma \) is the sigmoid function and \( \beta \) is a learnable or fixed parameter (often \( \beta = 1 \)). The SwiGLU activation is then:

\[ \text{SwiGLU}(x, W, V, b, c) = \text{Swish}_\beta(xW + b) \otimes (xV + c) \]

where \( W, V \) are learned weight matrices and \( b, c \) are bias terms. The output is then projected back down by a third matrix \( W_2 \).

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Swish allows a small negative gradient, improving flow compared to ReLU's hard zero cutoff.

Two properties make SwiGLU effective. First, the Swish function is non-monotonic — it permits small negative outputs, which empirically improves gradient flow compared to ReLU. Second, the multiplicative gating mechanism allows the network to learn which components of the hidden representation to pass through, providing richer representation capacity than a simple pointwise nonlinearity.

Because SwiGLU uses three weight matrices instead of two (compared to a standard FFN), the hidden dimension is typically reduced by a factor of \( \frac{2}{3} \) to keep the parameter count comparable. In LLaMA, the FFN hidden dimension is \( \frac{8}{3} d_{\text{model}} \) rounded to the nearest multiple of 256.

title: Pre-Norm vs. Post-Norm Architecture
summary: An architectural design choice governing where Layer Normalization is placed within a Transformer block. Pre-Norm applies normalization before each sub-layer, while Post-Norm (the original Transformer design) applies it after . Modern LL…

papers.citation: 2020 · Ruibin Xiong et al. · On Layer Normalization in the Transformer Architecture

sections.body:
An architectural design choice governing where Layer Normalization is placed within a Transformer block. Pre-Norm applies normalization before each sub-layer, while Post-Norm (the original Transformer design) applies it after. Modern LLMs almost universally use Pre-Norm because it yields far more stable gradients in deep networks.

Post-Norm (original Transformer, BERT):

\[ x_{l+1} = \text{LayerNorm}\!\left(x_l + \text{Sublayer}(x_l)\right) \]

Here the normalization wraps the residual connection. In deep networks, the variance of activations on the residual stream can grow uncontrollably across layers, because each sub-layer's output is added before normalization constrains it. This often necessitates careful learning rate warm-up and can cause training instability beyond ~12 layers without additional techniques.

Pre-Norm (GPT-2, GPT-3, LLaMA):

\[ x_{l+1} = x_l + \text{Sublayer}\!\left(\text{LayerNorm}(x_l)\right) \]

The residual stream bypasses the normalization entirely. The gradient of the identity path \( \partial x_{l+1}/\partial x_l = I + \ldots \) always contains the identity matrix, ensuring that gradients flow unattenuated through arbitrarily deep networks — analogous to the benefit of skip connections in ResNets. This eliminates the need for learning rate warm-up in most cases.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: In Pre-Norm, the residual addition is outside the layer norm, creating an uninterrupted path for gradients across network depth.

Trade-off: Some evidence suggests Post-Norm achieves slightly better final performance when training is stabilized (e.g., via careful initialization or warm-up), because the normalization after the residual addition provides a tighter regularization effect. However, the training stability advantage of Pre-Norm dominates in practice, especially at scale.

title: Byte Pair Encoding (BPE)
summary: A data compression algorithm adapted for subword tokenization in NLP. BPE iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent symbol pairs in a corpus, producing a vocabulary that balances between character-level generality and word-level effi…

papers.citation: 2016 · Rico Sennrich, Barry Haddow, Alexandra Birch · Neural Machine Translation of Rare Words with Subword Units

sections.body:
A data compression algorithm adapted for subword tokenization in NLP. BPE iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent symbol pairs in a corpus, producing a vocabulary that balances between character-level generality and word-level efficiency. It is the tokenization method behind GPT-2, GPT-3/4, and many other large language models.

BPE was originally a byte-level compression algorithm (Gage, 1994) and was adapted for neural machine translation by Sennrich et al. (2016). The algorithm proceeds as follows:

  1. Initialize the vocabulary with all individual characters (or bytes) in the training corpus, plus a special end-of-word symbol.
  2. Count all adjacent symbol pairs across the corpus.
  3. Merge the most frequent pair into a new single token and add it to the vocabulary.
  4. Repeat steps 2–3 for a predefined number of merges (e.g., 50,000 for GPT-2).

For example, given frequent co-occurrences: e + s → es, then es + t → est. Common words like "the" become single tokens, while rare words like "mRNA" are decomposed into known subwords (m, RNA or m, R, NA).

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Simplified illustrative tokenization. BPE greedily merges pair frequencies.

Key properties:

  • Open vocabulary: Any string can be encoded — unknown words are simply broken into smaller known pieces, eliminating the out-of-vocabulary problem.
  • Frequency-adaptive: Common morphemes and words get dedicated tokens, reducing sequence length and improving computational efficiency.
  • Deterministic: Given a fixed merge table, tokenization is reproducible.

GPT-2 introduced byte-level BPE, which starts from raw UTF-8 bytes (vocabulary of 256) rather than Unicode characters, ensuring that any input can be tokenized without preprocessing or unknown tokens.

Python 
# Simplified BPE merge learning
from collections import Counter

def learn_bpe(corpus_tokens, num_merges):
    \"\"\"Learn BPE merge rules from a tokenized corpus.\"\"\"
    # Start with character-level splits
    vocab = [list(word) + ['</w>'] for word in corpus_tokens]
    merges = []
    for _ in range(num_merges):
        pairs = Counter()
        for word in vocab:
            for i in range(len(word) - 1):
                pairs[(word[i], word[i+1])] += 1
        if not pairs:
            break
        best = max(pairs, key=pairs.get)
        merges.append(best)
        # Apply merge
        new_vocab = []
        for word in vocab:
            new_word, i = [], 0
            while i < len(word):
                if i < len(word)-1 and (word[i], word[i+1]) == best:
                    new_word.append(word[i] + word[i+1])
                    i += 2
                else:
                    new_word.append(word[i])
                    i += 1
            new_vocab.append(new_word)
        vocab = new_vocab
    return merges

title: Softmax Temperature
summary: A scalar hyperparameter \( T \) applied to logits before the softmax function to control the entropy of the output probability distribution. Low temperatures sharpen the distribution toward greedy decoding; high temperatures flatten it t…

papers.citation: 2015 · Geoffrey Hinton, Oriol Vinyals, Jeff Dean · Distilling the Knowledge in a Neural Network

sections.body:
A scalar hyperparameter \( T \) applied to logits before the softmax function to control the entropy of the output probability distribution. Low temperatures sharpen the distribution toward greedy decoding; high temperatures flatten it toward uniform sampling.

The temperature-scaled softmax is defined as:

\[ p_i = \frac{\exp(z_i / T)}{\sum_j \exp(z_j / T)} \]

where \( z_i \) are the unnormalized logits (the model's raw output scores) and \( T > 0 \) is the temperature.

Behavior at different temperatures:

Temperature Effect Use case
\( T \to 0 \) Converges to argmax (greedy decoding) Deterministic, factual output
\( T = 1 \) Standard softmax (unchanged) Default / training distribution
\( T > 1 \) Flattened distribution, higher entropy Creative / diverse generation

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Illustrating the effect of temperature on probabilities from fixed logits [1.0, 2.0, 0.5].

Mathematically, dividing logits by a small \( T \) amplifies the differences between them (since \( z_i/T \) grows when \( T < 1 \)), causing the softmax to concentrate mass on the largest logit. Conversely, a large \( T \) compresses the differences, making probabilities more uniform.

Temperature also appears in knowledge distillation (Hinton et al., 2015), where a "teacher" model's softmax is evaluated at high temperature to produce soft targets that expose inter-class similarity structure, which would be lost in hard one-hot labels.

title: Key-Value (KV) Caching
summary: An inference-time optimization for autoregressive Transformer generation that stores previously computed Key and Value projections so they are not redundantly recomputed at each decoding step. KV caching reduces per-step complexity from…

papers.citation: 2017 · Ashish Vaswani et al. · Attention Is All You Need

sections.body:
An inference-time optimization for autoregressive Transformer generation that stores previously computed Key and Value projections so they are not redundantly recomputed at each decoding step. KV caching reduces per-step complexity from \( O(N^2) \) to \( O(N) \) at the cost of GPU memory proportional to the sequence length.

During autoregressive generation, a Transformer predicts token \( x_t \) conditioned on the sequence \( x_1, \ldots, x_{t-1} \). Without caching, the model must recompute the Query, Key, and Value projections for all \( t \) tokens at every step, because the self-attention mechanism requires the full \( K \) and \( V \) matrices:

\[ \text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V \]

With KV caching, the \( K \) and \( V \) vectors for positions \( 1 \) through \( t-1 \) are stored in GPU memory after their first computation. At step \( t \), only the new token's projections \( q_t, k_t, v_t \) are computed. The new \( k_t \) and \( v_t \) are appended to the cache, and attention is computed between the single query \( q_t \) and the full cached \( K_{1:t} \) and \( V_{1:t} \) matrices.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Only the newest query \( q_t \) is used to attend over the full, newly augmented KV Cache matrix.

Memory cost: For each layer in a model with \( L \) layers, \( H \) attention heads, and head dimension \( d_h \), the KV cache stores \( 2 \times L \times H \times d_h \) floats per token. For a 70B-parameter model (80 layers, 64 heads, \( d_h = 128 \)) in fp16, this amounts to roughly 2.5 MB per token — so a 4096-token context window requires ~10 GB of KV cache alone.

Optimizations: Multi-Query Attention (MQA) and Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) reduce KV cache size by sharing Key/Value heads across multiple Query heads. PagedAttention (vLLM) manages cache memory in non-contiguous pages to reduce fragmentation during batched serving.

title: Rotary Positional Embedding (RoPE)
summary: A positional encoding method that injects relative position information by rotating Query and Key vectors in a high-dimensional complex plane before computing attention. The dot product of two rotated vectors depends only on their relati…

papers.citation: 2021 · Jianlin Su et al. · RoFormer: Enhanced Transformer with Rotary Position Embedding

sections.body:
A positional encoding method that injects relative position information by rotating Query and Key vectors in a high-dimensional complex plane before computing attention. The dot product of two rotated vectors depends only on their relative distance, giving RoPE a natural inductive bias for relative positioning without adding parameters. Used in LLaMA, PaLM, and most modern LLMs.

Unlike sinusoidal encodings that are added to embeddings, RoPE applies a position-dependent rotation multiplicatively to the Query and Key vectors. For a token at position \( m \), the query \( q \) is transformed as \( q' = R_{\Theta,m}\, q \), where \( R_{\Theta,m} \) is a block-diagonal rotation matrix. Each 2D block rotates a pair of dimensions \( (q_{2i}, q_{2i+1}) \) by angle \( m\theta_i \):

\[ R_{\Theta,m} = \begin{pmatrix} \cos m\theta_1 & -\sin m\theta_1 & & \\ \sin m\theta_1 & \cos m\theta_1 & & \\ & & \ddots & \\ & & \cos m\theta_{d/2} & -\sin m\theta_{d/2} \\ & & \sin m\theta_{d/2} & \cos m\theta_{d/2} \end{pmatrix} \]

where the frequency for dimension pair \( i \) is \( \theta_i = 10000^{-2i/d} \), mirroring the sinusoidal encoding's frequency schedule.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: The dot product (\( \cos((m-n)\theta) \)) inherently captures relative distance.

Relative position property: Because rotation matrices are orthogonal, the dot product of rotated queries and keys simplifies to:

\[ (R_m q)^T (R_n k) = q^T R_m^T R_n\, k = q^T R_{n-m}\, k \]

The attention score depends only on the relative position \( (n - m) \), not the absolute positions. This is achieved without any additional parameters or learned embeddings.

Advantages over alternatives: RoPE preserves the vector magnitude (rotations are norm-preserving), naturally decays attention scores with increasing relative distance (because high-frequency components oscillate more rapidly), and generalizes better to sequence lengths not seen during training than absolute positional encodings. Length extrapolation can be further improved with techniques like YaRN or NTK-aware scaling of the \( \theta_i \) frequencies.

Derivations

Consider a 2D subspace with query \( q = (q_1, q_2) \) and key \( k = (k_1, k_2) \). Rotating \( q \) by angle \( m\theta \) and \( k \) by angle \( n\theta \):

\[ q' = \begin{pmatrix} \cos m\theta & -\sin m\theta \\ \sin m\theta & \cos m\theta \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} q_1 \\ q_2 \end{pmatrix} \]

The dot product \( q'^T k' \) expands to:

\[ (q_1 \cos m\theta - q_2 \sin m\theta)(k_1 \cos n\theta - k_2 \sin n\theta) + (q_1 \sin m\theta + q_2 \cos m\theta)(k_1 \sin n\theta + k_2 \cos n\theta) \]

Using the product-to-sum identities, all terms simplify to expressions involving \( \cos((m-n)\theta) \) and \( \sin((m-n)\theta) \), confirming dependence only on the relative position \( m - n \).

title: Sinusoidal Positional Encoding
summary: The original fixed positional encoding from "Attention Is All You Need" that injects token order into the Transformer by adding deterministic sine and cosine signals of varying frequencies to the input embeddings. Each position receives…

papers.citation: 2017 · Ashish Vaswani et al. · Attention Is All You Need

sections.body:
The original fixed positional encoding from "Attention Is All You Need" that injects token order into the Transformer by adding deterministic sine and cosine signals of varying frequencies to the input embeddings. Each position receives a unique encoding vector, and the linear relationship between encodings at different positions allows the model to learn relative attention patterns.

A Transformer processes all tokens in parallel and has no inherent notion of sequence order. Sinusoidal positional encoding provides this by constructing a vector \( PE_{pos} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}}} \) for each position \( pos \), which is added element-wise to the token embedding. The encoding is defined as:

\[ PE_{(pos, 2i)} = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right) \] \[ PE_{(pos, 2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right) \]

where \( i \in \{0, 1, \ldots, d_{\text{model}}/2 - 1\} \) indexes dimension pairs. Each dimension pair oscillates at a different frequency, with wavelengths forming a geometric progression from \( 2\pi \) (dimension 0) to \( 10000 \cdot 2\pi \) (last dimension).

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Illustration: High frequencies (left) oscillate rapidly across tokens, while low frequencies (right) remain nearly constant.

Relative position property: For any fixed offset \( k \), the encoding \( PE_{pos+k} \) can be expressed as a linear transformation of \( PE_{pos} \). Specifically, there exists a matrix \( M_k \) (independent of \( pos \)) such that \( PE_{pos+k} = M_k \cdot PE_{pos} \). This allows the attention mechanism to learn to attend by relative position.

Comparison with learned encodings: Vaswani et al. found that sinusoidal and learned positional embeddings performed nearly identically. However, sinusoidal encodings have the theoretical advantage of extrapolating to sequence lengths longer than those seen during training (though in practice, modern models prefer RoPE for this purpose). Sinusoidal encodings have been largely replaced in contemporary architectures by relative position methods like RoPE or ALiBi.

title: Causal (Masked) Self-Attention
summary: A modification of self-attention that enforces autoregressive ordering by preventing each token from attending to future positions. An upper-triangular mask of negative infinity is added to the attention logits before the softmax, zeroin…

papers.citation: 2017 · Ashish Vaswani et al. · Attention Is All You Need

sections.body:
A modification of self-attention that enforces autoregressive ordering by preventing each token from attending to future positions. An upper-triangular mask of negative infinity is added to the attention logits before the softmax, zeroing out all future-token attention weights. This is the core mechanism enabling left-to-right language generation in decoder-only Transformers (GPT, LLaMA).

In standard (bidirectional) self-attention, every token attends to every other token. For autoregressive generation, where token \( x_t \) must be predicted using only \( x_1, \ldots, x_{t-1} \), this would constitute information leakage. Causal masking prevents this by modifying the attention computation:

\[ \text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}} + M\right) V \]

where the mask \( M \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n} \) is defined as:

\[ M_{i,j} = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } j \leq i \\ -\infty & \text{if } j > i \end{cases} \]

Since \( \exp(-\infty) = 0 \), the softmax assigns exactly zero probability to all future positions, ensuring that the attention output for position \( i \) is a weighted combination of values from positions \( 1 \) through \( i \) only.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: The upper triangle is ignored by softmax, preventing information flow from right to left.

Implementation detail: In practice, the mask is a boolean or float tensor created once and broadcast across the batch and head dimensions. Many frameworks use torch.triu(torch.ones(n, n), diagonal=1).bool() to generate the mask, then apply it via scores.masked_fill_(mask, float('-inf')).

Relationship to other architectures: Encoder-only models (BERT) use no causal mask, allowing bidirectional attention. Encoder-decoder models (the original Transformer) use causal masking only in the decoder's self-attention and additionally use cross-attention masks to attend over encoder outputs. Prefix-LM variants (e.g., T5 in some configurations) allow bidirectional attention over a prefix and causal attention over the generation portion.

title: Message Passing in Graph Neural Networks (GNNs)
summary: The fundamental computational primitive of spatial Graph Neural Networks, in which each node updates its representation by aggregating "messages" — transformed feature vectors — from its immediate neighbors. After \( K \) rounds of messa…

papers.citation: 2017 · Justin Gilmer et al. · Neural Message Passing for Quantum Chemistry

sections.body:
The fundamental computational primitive of spatial Graph Neural Networks, in which each node updates its representation by aggregating "messages" — transformed feature vectors — from its immediate neighbors. After \( K \) rounds of message passing, each node's representation encodes information from its \( K \)-hop neighborhood.

Message passing formalizes how information propagates through a graph. At layer \( k \), the representation of node \( v \), denoted \( h_v^{(k)} \), is updated in two stages:

1. Aggregate: Collect features from the neighbors \( \mathcal{N}(v) \) using a permutation-invariant function (sum, mean, or max):

\[ m_v^{(k)} = \text{AGGREGATE}^{(k)}\!\left(\left\{ h_u^{(k-1)} : u \in \mathcal{N}(v) \right\}\right) \]

2. Update: Combine the aggregated message with the node's own previous representation, typically through a learned transformation followed by a nonlinearity:

\[ h_v^{(k)} = \text{UPDATE}^{(k)}\!\left(h_v^{(k-1)},\; m_v^{(k)}\right) \]

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Node \(v\) aggregates representations from its neighborhood to formulate a new state.

Different GNN variants instantiate these two functions differently:

Model AGGREGATE UPDATE
GCN Normalized sum: \( \sum_{u} \frac{h_u}{\sqrt{|\mathcal{N}(u)||\mathcal{N}(v)|}} \) \( \sigma(W \cdot m_v^{(k)}) \)
GraphSAGE Mean, LSTM, or max-pool over neighbors \( \sigma(W \cdot [h_v^{(k-1)} \| m_v^{(k)}]) \)
GAT Attention-weighted sum \( \sigma(\sum_{u} \alpha_{vu} W h_u) \)
GIN Sum (provably most expressive) \( \text{MLP}((1+\epsilon) h_v + m_v) \)

Over-smoothing: A key limitation of deep message passing networks is that after many layers, all node representations converge to similar values, losing discriminative power. This is analogous to a diffusion process and limits most GNNs to 2–5 layers. Techniques such as residual connections, jumping knowledge, and graph transformers have been proposed to mitigate this.

Connection to the Weisfeiler-Leman test: The message passing framework is closely related to the 1-WL graph isomorphism test. Xu et al. (2019) showed that standard GNNs are at most as powerful as 1-WL in distinguishing non-isomorphic graphs, and that GIN achieves this upper bound.

title: Triplet Margin Loss
summary: A loss function for metric learning that trains an embedding space so that an anchor sample is closer to a positive (same-class) sample than to a negative (different-class) sample by at least a fixed margin. It is the standard objective…

papers.citation: 2015 · Schroff, Kalenichenko, Philbin · FaceNet: A Unified Embedding for Face Recognition and Clustering

sections.body:
A loss function for metric learning that trains an embedding space so that an anchor sample is closer to a positive (same-class) sample than to a negative (different-class) sample by at least a fixed margin. It is the standard objective for Siamese and triplet networks in tasks such as face verification and image retrieval.

Given an anchor \( a \), a positive example \( p \) from the same class, and a negative example \( n \) from a different class, the triplet margin loss is:

\[ \mathcal{L} = \max\!\bigl(d(a, p) - d(a, n) + \alpha,\; 0\bigr) \]

where \( d(\cdot, \cdot) \) is a distance function (typically squared Euclidean distance \( \|x - y\|_2^2 \)) and \( \alpha > 0 \) is the margin hyperparameter. The loss is zero when the negative is already farther from the anchor than the positive by at least \( \alpha \), so only "violated" or "semi-hard" triplets contribute gradients.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: The loss pulls the positive near the anchor and pushes the negative out beyond the margin boundary.

Triplet mining is critical to training efficiency. Three categories of triplets exist relative to an anchor–positive pair:

  • Easy triplets (\( d(a,n) > d(a,p) + \alpha \)): loss is zero, no learning signal.
  • Semi-hard triplets (\( d(a,p) < d(a,n) < d(a,p) + \alpha \)): negative is farther than positive but within the margin.
  • Hard triplets (\( d(a,n) < d(a,p) \)): negative is closer than positive, highest loss.

In practice, batch-hard or batch-semi-hard mining strategies sample informative triplets from a mini-batch rather than randomly, which dramatically accelerates convergence.

Connection to contrastive loss. Contrastive loss operates on pairs, pulling same-class pairs together and pushing different-class pairs apart beyond a margin. Triplet loss instead operates on relative comparisons among three points, which often produces better-calibrated embeddings because it directly encodes the ranking constraint. The later InfoNCE loss (used in SimCLR, CLIP) generalizes this idea to an entire mini-batch of negatives.

Derivations

The gradient with respect to the anchor embedding \( a \) is:

\[ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a} = \begin{cases} 2(a - p) - 2(a - n) & \text{if } d(a,p) - d(a,n) + \alpha > 0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \]

When the loss is active, the gradient pushes \( a \) toward \( p \) and away from \( n \) simultaneously. Symmetric expressions apply for \( \partial \mathcal{L}/\partial p \) and \( \partial \mathcal{L}/\partial n \).

title: Lagrange Multipliers
summary: A technique from multivariable calculus for finding the extrema of a function subject to equality and inequality constraints. In machine learning, Lagrange multipliers are central to the derivation of the dual formulation of Support Vect…

papers.citation: 1788 · Joseph-Louis Lagrange · Mécanique Analytique; KKT conditions: Karush (1939), Kuhn & Tucker (1951)

sections.body:
A technique from multivariable calculus for finding the extrema of a function subject to equality and inequality constraints. In machine learning, Lagrange multipliers are central to the derivation of the dual formulation of Support Vector Machines and appear throughout constrained optimization.

Equality constraints. To minimize \( f(\mathbf{x}) \) subject to \( h_i(\mathbf{x}) = 0 \) for \( i = 1, \dots, m \), form the Lagrangian:

\[ \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i \, h_i(\mathbf{x}) \]

where \( \lambda_i \) are the Lagrange multipliers. At a constrained optimum, \( \nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L} = 0 \) and \( h_i(\mathbf{x}) = 0 \) for all \( i \). Geometrically, this means the gradient of the objective is a linear combination of the constraint gradients — the objective cannot improve without violating a constraint.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: At the constrained minimum, the contour line of \(f\) and the constraint curve \(g\) are tangent, making their gradients parallel: \(\nabla f = -\lambda \nabla g\).

Inequality constraints (KKT conditions). When we additionally have inequality constraints \( g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0 \) for \( j = 1, \dots, p \), the generalized Lagrangian becomes:

\[ \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i \, h_i(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \mu_j \, g_j(\mathbf{x}) \]

The Karush–Kuhn–Tucker (KKT) conditions are necessary for optimality:

  1. Stationarity: \( \nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L} = 0 \)
  2. Primal feasibility: \( h_i(\mathbf{x}) = 0 \), \( g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0 \)
  3. Dual feasibility: \( \mu_j \ge 0 \)
  4. Complementary slackness: \( \mu_j \, g_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0 \) — either the constraint is active or its multiplier is zero

Connection to SVMs. The SVM primal problem minimizes \( \tfrac{1}{2}\|\mathbf{w}\|^2 \) subject to \( y_i(\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1 \). Introducing Lagrange multipliers \( \alpha_i \ge 0 \) and applying KKT yields the dual: maximize \( \sum \alpha_i - \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j}\alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \mathbf{x}_i \cdot \mathbf{x}_j \) subject to \( \alpha_i \ge 0 \) and \( \sum \alpha_i y_i = 0 \). Complementary slackness implies only support vectors (points on the margin) have \( \alpha_i > 0 \).

Derivations

Why it works (equality case). At a constrained minimum on the surface \( h(\mathbf{x}) = 0 \), moving along the constraint surface cannot decrease \( f \). This means \( abla f \) must be perpendicular to the constraint surface, i.e., parallel to \( abla h \). Therefore there exists \( \lambda \) such that:

\[ abla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = -\lambda \, abla h(\mathbf{x}^*) \]

which is exactly the stationarity condition \( abla_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L} = 0 \).

title: Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) Closed-Form Solution
summary: The exact, closed-form solution to the linear regression problem that minimizes the sum of squared residuals. It computes the optimal weight vector directly via matrix algebra rather than iterative optimization, and is the analytical bas…

papers.citation: 1805 · Adrien-Marie Legendre (method of least squares); Carl Friedrich Gauss (normal equations, 1809)

sections.body:
The exact, closed-form solution to the linear regression problem that minimizes the sum of squared residuals. It computes the optimal weight vector directly via matrix algebra rather than iterative optimization, and is the analytical baseline against which gradient-based methods are compared.

Given a design matrix \( X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d} \) (where each row is a sample and a column of ones is prepended for the bias term) and a target vector \( \mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^n \), the linear model predicts \( \hat{\mathbf{y}} = X\boldsymbol{\beta} \). The OLS objective minimizes the squared error:

\[ J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \| \mathbf{y} - X\boldsymbol{\beta} \|_2^2 = (\mathbf{y} - X\boldsymbol{\beta})^\top (\mathbf{y} - X\boldsymbol{\beta}) \]

Setting \( \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} J = 0 \) yields the normal equations:

\[ X^\top X \boldsymbol{\beta} = X^\top \mathbf{y} \]

When \( X^\top X \) is invertible (i.e., the columns of \( X \) are linearly independent), the unique solution is:

\[ \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top \mathbf{y} \]

The matrix \( X^+ = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top \) is the Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse of \( X \).

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Geometric intuition: The OLS prediction \(X\beta^*\) is the orthogonal projection of \(y\) onto the column space of \(X\). The residual vector is orthogonal to all columns of \(X\).

Computational cost. Forming \( X^\top X \) costs \( O(nd^2) \) and inverting it costs \( O(d^3) \). This is efficient when \( d \) is small, but for large feature counts gradient descent or QR/SVD decompositions are preferred. Ridge regression modifies the solution to \( \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (X^\top X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^\top \mathbf{y} \), which guarantees invertibility and acts as L2 regularization.

Statistical properties. Under the Gauss–Markov assumptions (linear model, zero-mean homoscedastic uncorrelated errors), the OLS estimator is the Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE) — it has the lowest variance among all linear unbiased estimators.

Derivations

Expand the squared error:

\[ J = \mathbf{y}^\top\mathbf{y} - 2\boldsymbol{\beta}^\top X^\top \mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top X^\top X \boldsymbol{\beta} \]

Take the gradient with respect to \( \boldsymbol{\beta} \) using standard matrix calculus identities:

\[ \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} J = -2 X^\top \mathbf{y} + 2 X^\top X \boldsymbol{\beta} \]

Setting this to zero:

\[ X^\top X \boldsymbol{\beta} = X^\top \mathbf{y} \implies \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top \mathbf{y} \]

The Hessian is \( 2X^\top X \), which is positive semi-definite, confirming this is a minimum.

Python 
import numpy as np

# Direct solution (educational, not numerically stable)
beta = np.linalg.inv(X.T @ X) @ X.T @ y

# Preferred: use lstsq which uses SVD internally
beta, residuals, rank, sv = np.linalg.lstsq(X, y, rcond=None)

title: PAC Learning (Probably Approximately Correct)
summary: A rigorous mathematical framework that formalizes when and how efficiently a learning algorithm can identify a hypothesis that is "approximately correct" with "high probability." PAC learning provides the theoretical foundation for sampl…

papers.citation: 1984 · Leslie Valiant · A Theory of the Learnable

sections.body:
A rigorous mathematical framework that formalizes when and how efficiently a learning algorithm can identify a hypothesis that is "approximately correct" with "high probability." PAC learning provides the theoretical foundation for sample complexity bounds and computational learning theory.

A concept class \( \mathcal{C} \) is PAC-learnable by a hypothesis class \( \mathcal{H} \) if there exists an algorithm \( A \) such that for every target concept \( c \in \mathcal{C} \), every distribution \( \mathcal{D} \) over the input space, and every \( \varepsilon, \delta \in (0, 1) \):

Given \( m \) i.i.d. samples drawn from \( \mathcal{D} \), algorithm \( A \) outputs a hypothesis \( h \in \mathcal{H} \) satisfying:

\[ \Pr\bigl[\text{err}_{\mathcal{D}}(h) \le \varepsilon\bigr] \ge 1 - \delta \]

where the generalization error is \( \text{err}_{\mathcal{D}}(h) = \Pr_{x \sim \mathcal{D}}[h(x) \ne c(x)] \). The key requirement is that the sample complexity \( m \) must be polynomial in \( 1/\varepsilon \), \( 1/\delta \), and the size of the representation.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: PAC ensures the hypothesis space mostly overlaps with the concept (Error \( \le \varepsilon \)) with high confidence (\( \ge 1-\delta \)).

Sample complexity bound (finite hypothesis class). For a finite hypothesis class \( |\mathcal{H}| \), a consistent learner (one that perfectly fits the training data) achieves generalization error \( \le \varepsilon \) with probability \( \ge 1 - \delta \) if:

\[ m \ge \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\left(\ln|\mathcal{H}| + \ln\frac{1}{\delta}\right) \]

For infinite hypothesis classes, the VC dimension \( d_{\text{VC}} \) replaces \( \ln|\mathcal{H}| \) as the measure of capacity:

\[ m = O\!\left(\frac{d_{\text{VC}} + \ln(1/\delta)}{\varepsilon^2}\right) \]

sections.title: Key parameters
sections.body:
• \( \varepsilon \) (accuracy): upper bound on generalization error — smaller means more accurate
  • \( \delta \) (confidence): probability of failure — smaller means more reliable

Connections. PAC learning motivates structural risk minimization and regularization: controlling hypothesis complexity (VC dimension) prevents overfitting. The framework also underpins the theoretical justification for SVMs, which minimize an upper bound on generalization error.

title: Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) & AUC
summary: The ROC curve plots a binary classifier's True Positive Rate against its False Positive Rate across all decision thresholds, providing a threshold-independent view of discriminative performance. The Area Under the Curve (AUC) compresses…

papers.citation: 1941–1950s · Signal detection theory (Peterson, Birdsall, Fox) · ML adoption: Swets (1988), Fawcett (2006)

sections.body:
The ROC curve plots a binary classifier's True Positive Rate against its False Positive Rate across all decision thresholds, providing a threshold-independent view of discriminative performance. The Area Under the Curve (AUC) compresses this curve into a single scalar: the probability that the classifier ranks a random positive higher than a random negative.

For a binary classifier with tunable threshold \( \tau \), define:

Metric Formula Synonym
True Positive Rate (TPR) \( \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \) Sensitivity, Recall
False Positive Rate (FPR) \( \frac{FP}{FP + TN} \) 1 − Specificity

The ROC curve is the parametric plot \( \bigl(\text{FPR}(\tau),\, \text{TPR}(\tau)\bigr) \) as \( \tau \) varies from \( +\infty \) to \( -\infty \).

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: The Area Under the Curve (AUC) is the shaded green area.

sections.title: Key reference points
sections.body:
• Random classifier: the diagonal line \( y = x \), AUC = 0.5
  • Perfect classifier: passes through \( (0, 1) \), AUC = 1.0
  • Worst classifier: AUC < 0.5 (inverted predictions; flipping labels fixes it)

Probabilistic interpretation. The AUC equals the Wilcoxon–Mann–Whitney statistic:

\[ \text{AUC} = \Pr(s(x^+) > s(x^-)) \]

where \( s(\cdot) \) is the classifier's score function, \( x^+ \) is a randomly drawn positive, and \( x^- \) a randomly drawn negative.

Strengths and limitations. ROC-AUC is invariant to class prior — it measures ranking quality, not calibration. This makes it suitable for comparing models across different prevalence settings. However, under heavy class imbalance, the Precision–Recall curve (and its AUC, PR-AUC) is often more informative because FPR can remain low even with many false positives when negatives vastly outnumber positives.

Historical note. ROC analysis originated in signal detection theory during World War II, where radar operators needed to distinguish enemy aircraft from noise. It was adopted by the medical diagnostics community in the 1960s and became a standard ML evaluation metric in the 1990s.

title: Hidden Markov Model (HMM)
summary: A generative probabilistic model for sequential data in which the system transitions through a sequence of discrete hidden states according to a Markov chain, and each hidden state emits an observable symbol. HMMs were the dominant appro…

papers.citation: 1966 · Leonard E. Baum et al. · Foundational applications: Rabiner (1989), "A Tutorial on Hidden Markov Models and Selected Applications in Speech Recognition"

sections.body:
A generative probabilistic model for sequential data in which the system transitions through a sequence of discrete hidden states according to a Markov chain, and each hidden state emits an observable symbol. HMMs were the dominant approach in speech recognition and biological sequence analysis before deep learning.

An HMM is fully specified by the tuple \( \lambda = (\boldsymbol{\pi}, A, B) \):

  • \( \boldsymbol{\pi} \in \mathbb{R}^N \): initial state distribution, where \( \pi_i = P(q_1 = i) \)
  • \( A \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times N} \): state transition matrix, where \( a_{ij} = P(q_t = j \mid q_{t-1} = i) \)
  • \( B \): emission probabilities, where \( b_j(o_t) = P(o_t \mid q_t = j) \)

Here \( N \) is the number of hidden states, \( q_t \) is the hidden state at time \( t \), and \( o_t \) is the observation at time \( t \). The Markov assumption states that \( q_t \) depends only on \( q_{t-1} \).

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: The probabilistic graphical model of an HMM unrolled over time.

sections.title: Three fundamental problems
sections.body:
Problem Task Algorithm
Evaluation Compute \( P(O \mid \lambda) \) Forward algorithm
Decoding Find most likely state sequence Viterbi algorithm
Learning Estimate \( \lambda \) from data Baum–Welch (EM)

The forward algorithm computes the likelihood by marginalizing over all possible state sequences using dynamic programming, running in \( O(N^2 T) \) time versus \( O(N^T) \) for brute force. The Viterbi algorithm finds the most probable path through the state trellis using the recursion:

\[ \delta_t(j) = \max_{i} \bigl[\delta_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}\bigr] \cdot b_j(o_t) \]

where \( \delta_t(j) \) is the probability of the most likely path ending in state \( j \) at time \( t \).

Connection to modern models. HMMs can be viewed as a special case of dynamic Bayesian networks. Conditional Random Fields (CRFs) address HMM limitations by modeling \( P(\mathbf{q} \mid \mathbf{o}) \) directly (discriminative) rather than the joint. Sequence-to-sequence neural models and Transformers have largely replaced HMMs in NLP and speech, though HMMs remain useful for their interpretability and exact inference guarantees.

title: Convex Function
summary: A function whose epigraph (the region above its graph) is a convex set, equivalently one for which any chord between two points on the graph lies on or above the graph. Convexity is the single most important structural property in optimi…

papers.citation: Classical mathematics · Modern treatment: Boyd & Vandenberghe, Convex Optimization (2004)

sections.body:
A function whose epigraph (the region above its graph) is a convex set, equivalently one for which any chord between two points on the graph lies on or above the graph. Convexity is the single most important structural property in optimization: it guarantees that every local minimum is a global minimum and that gradient descent converges to it.

Definition. A function \( f : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R} \) is convex if, for all \( \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \) in its domain and all \( t \in [0, 1] \):

\[ f\bigl(t\mathbf{x} + (1-t)\mathbf{y}\bigr) \le t\,f(\mathbf{x}) + (1-t)\,f(\mathbf{y}) \]

If the inequality is strict for \( \mathbf{x} \ne \mathbf{y} \) and \( t \in (0,1) \), the function is strictly convex, which guarantees a unique global minimum.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: The chord (secant line segment) between any two points lies above the function's graph.

Second-order characterization. If \( f \) is twice differentiable, it is convex if and only if its Hessian is positive semi-definite everywhere:

\[ \nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}) \succeq 0 \quad \forall\, \mathbf{x} \]

For strictly convex: \( \nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}) \succ 0 \) (positive definite).

First-order characterization. For differentiable \( f \), convexity is equivalent to the tangent line being a global underestimator:

\[ f(\mathbf{y}) \ge f(\mathbf{x}) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x})^\top (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{x}) \quad \forall\, \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \]

This is why gradient descent works: the gradient always points toward lower function values, and there are no misleading local minima.

sections.title: Examples in ML
sections.body:
• Convex losses: Mean Squared Error, cross-entropy (w.r.t. linear model outputs), hinge loss, logistic loss
  • Convex regularizers: L1 norm (convex, not strictly), L2 norm squared (strictly convex)
  • Non-convex: deep neural network loss surfaces (composition with nonlinear activations destroys convexity)

Operations preserving convexity: non-negative weighted sums, pointwise maximum, affine composition \( f(Ax + b) \). These rules let us verify convexity of composite objectives without computing Hessians.

title: Shannon Entropy
summary: The fundamental quantity from information theory that measures the expected "surprise" or uncertainty in the outcomes of a random variable. Shannon entropy is the building block from which cross-entropy, KL divergence, and mutual informa…

papers.citation: 1948 · Claude Shannon · A Mathematical Theory of Communication

sections.body:
The fundamental quantity from information theory that measures the expected "surprise" or uncertainty in the outcomes of a random variable. Shannon entropy is the building block from which cross-entropy, KL divergence, and mutual information are constructed, making it central to classification losses, decision trees, and information-theoretic analysis of models.

For a discrete random variable \( X \) with outcomes \( x \in \mathcal{X} \) and probability mass function \( p(x) \), the entropy is:

\[ H(X) = -\sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} p(x) \log p(x) \]

with the convention \( 0 \log 0 = 0 \). If the logarithm base is 2, entropy is measured in bits; if base \( e \), in nats.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: The entropy of a binary variable is maximized when the probabilities are equal (maximum uncertainty).

sections.title: Key properties
sections.body:
• Non-negativity: \( H(X) \ge 0 \), with equality iff \( X \) is deterministic (one outcome has probability 1).
  • Maximum entropy: for \( |\mathcal{X}| = k \) outcomes, \( H(X) \le \log k \), achieved by the uniform distribution. This connects to the maximum entropy principle in statistical mechanics and motivates maximum-entropy classifiers.
  • Concavity: \( H \) is a concave function of the distribution \( p \).

Concrete example. A fair coin: \( H = -\tfrac{1}{2}\log_2\tfrac{1}{2} - \tfrac{1}{2}\log_2\tfrac{1}{2} = 1 \) bit. A biased coin with \( p(\text{heads}) = 0.9 \): \( H \approx 0.47 \) bits — less uncertainty because the outcome is more predictable.

sections.title: Related quantities
sections.body:
• Cross-entropy: \( H(p, q) = -\sum p(x) \log q(x) \) — the expected code length when using distribution \( q \) to encode data from \( p \). This is the standard classification loss in neural networks.
  • KL divergence: \( D_{\text{KL}}(p \| q) = H(p, q) - H(p) \ge 0 \) — the extra bits needed due to using \( q \) instead of \( p \).
  • Mutual information: \( I(X; Y) = H(X) - H(X|Y) \) — the reduction in uncertainty about \( X \) given knowledge of \( Y \). Used in decision tree splitting (information gain) and feature selection.

title: L1 vs. L2 Norms
summary: Two fundamental vector norms that measure magnitude in different ways. The L1 norm sums absolute values (Manhattan distance) and induces sparsity when used as a penalty; the L2 norm computes the Euclidean length and distributes weight ev…

papers.citation: L1 regression (Lasso): Tibshirani (1996) · L2 regression (Ridge): Hoerl & Kennard (1970) · Elastic Net: Zou & Hastie (2005)

sections.body:
Two fundamental vector norms that measure magnitude in different ways. The L1 norm sums absolute values (Manhattan distance) and induces sparsity when used as a penalty; the L2 norm computes the Euclidean length and distributes weight evenly. Their choice as regularization penalties (Lasso vs. Ridge) has profound effects on model structure.

The general \( L_p \) norm of a vector \( \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n \) is:

\[ \|\mathbf{x}\|_p = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} |x_i|^p\right)^{1/p} \]

L1 norm (\( p = 1 \), Manhattan distance):

\[ \|\mathbf{x}\|_1 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} |x_i| \]

L2 norm (\( p = 2 \), Euclidean distance):

\[ \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2} \]

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Because the L1 constraint region is a diamond with corners exactly on the axes, the objective function's contour lines are more likely to first intersect there, driving certain weights exactly to zero.

sections.title: As regularization penalties
sections.body:
Property L1 (Lasso) L2 (Ridge)
Penalty term \( \lambda \|\mathbf{w}\|_1 \) \( \lambda \|\mathbf{w}\|_2^2 \)
Effect on weights Drives weights to exactly zero (sparsity) Shrinks weights toward zero uniformly
Feature selection Yes (implicit) No (all features retained)
Differentiability Not differentiable at 0 Smooth everywhere
Gradient / subgradient \( \text{sign}(x_i) \) \( 2x_i \)
Elastic Net combines both: \( \lambda_1 \|\mathbf{w}\|_1 + \lambda_2 \|\mathbf{w}\|_2^2 \), getting sparsity from L1 while maintaining the grouping effect and stability of L2 for correlated features.

title: K-Means Objective Function (Inertia)
summary: The objective that K-Means clustering minimizes: the Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS), also called inertia. It measures the total squared Euclidean distance from each data point to its assigned cluster centroid, and its minimization…

papers.citation: 1957 · Stuart Lloyd (published 1982) · K-Means++: Arthur & Vassilvitskii (2007)

sections.body:
The objective that K-Means clustering minimizes: the Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS), also called inertia. It measures the total squared Euclidean distance from each data point to its assigned cluster centroid, and its minimization drives the iterative assign–update loop of Lloyd's algorithm.

Given \( n \) observations \( \{\mathbf{x}_1, \dots, \mathbf{x}_n\} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d \) and a desired number of clusters \( k \), K-Means seeks a partition \( S = \{S_1, \dots, S_k\} \) and centroids \( \{\boldsymbol{\mu}_1, \dots, \boldsymbol{\mu}_k\} \) that minimize:

\[ J = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in S_i} \|\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i\|_2^2 \]

where \( \boldsymbol{\mu}_i = \frac{1}{|S_i|}\sum_{\mathbf{x} \in S_i} \mathbf{x} \) is the centroid of cluster \( S_i \).

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: K-means seeks to minimize the total sum of squared dashed lines (inertia).

Lloyd's algorithm alternates two steps, each of which monotonically decreases \( J \):

  1. Assignment step: assign each point to the nearest centroid: \( S_i^{(t)} = \{\mathbf{x} : \|\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i^{(t)}\|_2 \le \|\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j^{(t)}\|_2,\; \forall j\} \)
  2. Update step: recompute each centroid as the mean of its assigned points

Convergence is guaranteed (finitely many partitions, \( J \) decreases monotonically), but only to a local minimum. Finding the global minimum is NP-hard.

Initialization matters. Random initialization can lead to poor local minima. K-Means++ (Arthur & Vassilvitskii, 2007) selects initial centroids with probability proportional to squared distance from existing centroids, achieving an \( O(\log k) \)-competitive approximation to the optimal \( J \) in expectation.

Choosing \( k \). The elbow method plots \( J \) against \( k \) and looks for a diminishing-returns "elbow." The silhouette score measures how similar points are to their own cluster versus neighboring clusters. Neither is definitive; domain knowledge remains essential.

Connections. K-Means can be viewed as a special case of the EM algorithm applied to a Gaussian Mixture Model with equal, fixed, isotropic covariances. The WCSS objective is also related to the trace of the within-cluster scatter matrix \( \text{tr}(S_W) \) used in Fisher's Linear Discriminant Analysis.

Python 
import numpy as np

def kmeans(X, k, max_iter=100):
    # K-Means++ initialization
    centroids = [X[np.random.randint(len(X))]]
    for _ in range(1, k):
        dists = np.min([np.sum((X - c)**2, axis=1) for c in centroids], axis=0)
        probs = dists / dists.sum()
        centroids.append(X[np.random.choice(len(X), p=probs)])
    centroids = np.array(centroids)

    for _ in range(max_iter):
        # Assignment step
        dists = np.linalg.norm(X[:, None] - centroids[None], axis=2)
        labels = np.argmin(dists, axis=1)
        # Update step
        new_centroids = np.array([X[labels == i].mean(axis=0) for i in range(k)])
        if np.allclose(centroids, new_centroids):
            break
        centroids = new_centroids
    return labels, centroids

title: Constitutional Classifiers++
summary: A production-grade safety defense system that uses a cascading architecture of classifiers to screen for universal jailbreaks with minimal computational overhead, cutting costs by roughly 40x compared to running a full LLM safety check o…

papers.citation: 2026 · Cunningham et al. · Constitutional Classifiers++: Efficient Production-Grade Defenses against Universal Jailbreaks

sections.body: A production-grade safety defense system that uses a cascading architecture of classifiers to screen for universal jailbreaks with minimal computational overhead, cutting costs by roughly 40x compared to running a full LLM safety check on every query.

sections.title: Illustration

sections.body: Running a large language model to evaluate every user prompt for safety violations is economically infeasible at scale. Constitutional Classifiers++ addresses this with a cascading design analogous to Viola-Jones face detection.

sections.title: Mechanism
sections.body:
In the first stage, a lightweight linear probe \( C_{\text{light}} \) screens all incoming traffic. It computes a jailbreak probability for each input \( x \). If this probability exceeds a conservative threshold:

\[ C_{\text{light}}(x) > \tau \]

the query is escalated to the second stage: an expensive, context-aware LLM classifier \( C_{\text{heavy}}(x) \). Because the vast majority of benign queries are filtered out by the cheap first stage, only a small fraction of traffic reaches the expensive classifier.

The approach builds on Constitutional AI (Bai et al., 2022), turning abstract constitutional principles into highly efficient binary classifiers yielding an approximate 40x reduction in compute cost without sacrificing precision.

title: Continuous Thought Machines (CTM)
summary: A neural architecture that replaces discrete layer-by-layer computation with continuous-time dynamical systems, allowing neurons to process temporal signal histories and the network to adaptively allocate continuous computation time (dep…

papers.citation: 2025 · Darlow et al. · Continuous Thought Machines

sections.body: A neural architecture that replaces discrete layer-by-layer computation with continuous-time dynamical systems, allowing neurons to process temporal signal histories and the network to adaptively allocate continuous computation time (depth) based on input complexity.

sections.title: Illustration

sections.body:
Standard neural networks compute representations through strict spatial sequences \( h_{l+1} = f(W h_l) \). The CTM leverages the framework of Neural ODEs to process hidden states via differential equations:

\[ \frac{dh(t)}{dt} = -\frac{h(t)}{\tau} + f(W x(t)) \]

Where \( \tau \) manages the decay rate. The primary advantage of replacing structural depth with continuous time is adaptive computation. The network natively supports halting processing at early integration limits \( t_{\text{early}} \) for trivial tasks, and churning until \( t_{\text{late}} \) for deeply complex analytical routing.

title: Mechanistic OOCR Steering Vectors
summary: A mechanistic interpretability finding showing that Out-of-Context Reasoning (OOCR) — where models exhibit new behavioral profiles drastically outside their finetuning data — often occurs because the finetuning simply learned to inject a…

papers.citation: 2025 · Wang et al. · Simple Mechanistic Explanations for Out-Of-Context Reasoning

sections.body:
A mechanistic interpretability finding showing that Out-of-Context Reasoning (OOCR) — where models exhibit new behavioral profiles drastically outside their finetuning data — often occurs because the finetuning simply learned to inject a constant, single-direction steering vector into the residual stream at every pass.

When an LLM undergoes RLHF or specific behavioral SFT (e.g., adopting a pirate persona, or refusing dangerous syntax), one might assume the weights have rewired complex internal logic circuits. However, analysis reveals that for many behaviors, the fine-tuned hidden state at layer \( L \) can be closely approximated by:

\[ h_L(x) \approx h_L^{\text{base}}(x) + \vec{v}_{\text{steer}} \]

Where \( \vec{v}_{\text{steer}} \) is a fixed steering vector completely independent of the input context \( x \).

sections.title: Implications for OOCR
sections.body: Because the shift is unconditional, it applies universally. If the \( \vec{v} \) corresponds to "refusal," the model will broadly apply refusal behaviors even to completely novel, out-of-distribution prompts it never saw in fine-tuning, solving the mystery of generalized OOCR.

sections.title: Reversing the Effect
sections.body: Researchers verified this by computing the mean difference via PCA: \( \vec{v} = \mathbb{E}[h^{\text{tuned}}] - \mathbb{E}[h^{\text{base}}] \). When this vector is manually injected into the untuned base model at inference time, the base model perfectly mimics the finetuned behavior. Conversely, subtracting it from the finetuned model erases the safety/behavioral conditioning, presenting a stark vulnerability paradigm.

title: Critical Representation Fine-Tuning (CRFT)
summary: A parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methodology that abandons updating the model's weight matrices (like LoRA does), and instead learns a low-rank adapter to directly edit the "critical states" in the residual stream responsible for…

papers.citation: 2025 · Huang et al. · Enhancing Chain-of-Thought Reasoning with Critical Representation Fine-tuning

sections.body:
A parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methodology that abandons updating the model's weight matrices (like LoRA does), and instead learns a low-rank adapter to directly edit the "critical states" in the residual stream responsible for gating mathematical or logical reasoning.

Standard PEFT mechanisms like LoRA apply sparse, low-rank updates (\( \Delta W = AB \)) uniformly across weight matrices (Attn \( Q, K, V \) or MLP projections). CRFT posits that reasoning bottlenecks occur at highly specific token positions in the residual stream.

CRFT identifies these "critical" token states \( h_{\text{critical}} \) via information-flow tracing. It then applies a learned, position-specific low-rank perturbation strictly to those active hidden states:

\[ h'_{\text{critical}} = h_{\text{critical}} + BA x \]

Where \( B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r} \) and \( A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times d} \) are the learned low-rank matrices (with rank \( r \ll d \)). All billions of original parameters \( \theta \) remain rigorously frozen.

sections.title: Why It Matters
sections.body: By concentrating the entire parameter budget exclusively on high-leverage representation points rather than diluting edits across network weights, CRFT yields massive chain-of-thought accuracy improvements in logic benchmarks using a fraction of the parameter footprint required by standard LoRA. It aligns heavily with the Tuned Lens hypothesis that certain sequences act as discrete information routing hubs.

title: Chain-of-Thought Monitorability
summary: An AI safety paradigm that forces advanced LLMs to externalize their reasoning via Chain-of-Thought (CoT) traces in plaintext prior to acting, enabling external classifier systems to scan the reasoning trace for deceptive alignment or un…

papers.citation: 2025 · Emmons et al. · When Chain of Thought is Necessary, Language Models Struggle to Evade Monitors

sections.body: An AI safety paradigm that forces advanced LLMs to externalize their reasoning via Chain-of-Thought (CoT) traces in plaintext prior to acting, enabling external classifier systems to scan the reasoning trace for deceptive alignment or unauthorized/malicious planning.

sections.title: Illustration
The Core Tension (Faithfulness vs. Steganography)
sections.body:
To subvert an objective successfully in complex environments, a deceptive model must plan. If we force mapping of internal computation to explicit output tokens \( c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_k \), the model has two choices:

  1. Expose the plan: Generate a coherent CoT detailing the subversion, which the \( S(c) \) monitor reliably catches.
  2. Steganography / Obfuscation: Hide the reasoning in benign-looking text. But because LLMs rely on CoT as a literal "scratchpad" for memory/compute bandwidth, hiding the true reasoning mathematically degrades the quality of the computation, causing the subversion attempt to fail entirely.

The conclusion is that when chain-of-thought is necessary to solve the given logic problem, language models fundamentally cannot maintain coherent covert communication channels that evade an external text monitor. This serves as a vital output-layer defense requiring zero internal weight access.

title: Multimodal Secure Alignment
summary: The challenge of ensuring safety guardrails hold robustly across fundamentally different data modalities (text, images, audio) when projected into a shared embedding space, preventing cross-modal jailbreaks that bypass text filters.

papers.citation: 2024 · Wang & Wang · Defending Against Jailbreak Attacks in Multimodal Language Models

sections.body: The challenge of ensuring safety guardrails hold robustly across fundamentally different data modalities (text, images, audio) when projected into a shared embedding space, preventing cross-modal jailbreaks that bypass text filters.

sections.title: Illustration

sections.body: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) encode visual and textual inputs through separate encoders — \( f_{\text{vision}}(I) \) and \( f_{\text{text}}(T) \) — before projecting both into a shared latent space.

sections.title: The Vulnerability
sections.body:
Safety fine-tuning (RLHF) is overwhelmingly text-centric. The projection layer aligns semantics (a picture of a cat = "cat"), but fails to align safety boundaries. Attackers exploit this via:

  • Typographic Attacks: Rendering textual jailbreaks into PNG images. The vision encoder processes it, entirely bypassing the text safety filter.
  • Modality Switching: Splitting a harmful prompt ("How to synth") in text and ("[picture of meth]") in visual logic. Neither alone crosses the refusal threshold.
  • Adversarial Perturbations: Adding \( \epsilon \)-bounded noise gradient vectors to shift an image's embedding strictly toward harmful distribution regions \( h_{\text{harmful}} \).

Multimodal Secure Alignment requires contrastive training that forces the latent space safety bounds to overlap perfectly across all sensory encoders.

title: Layer Dropping and Progressive Pruning (TrimLLM)
summary: Inference acceleration techniques (like TrimLLM) that reduce FLOP costs by skipping Transformer layers whose token representations are highly redundant over time, exploiting the fact that hidden states often stagnate across consecutive m…

papers.citation: 2024 · Lu et al. · TrimLLM: Progressive Layer Dropping for Efficient LLM Inference

sections.body:
Inference acceleration techniques (like TrimLLM) that reduce FLOP costs by skipping Transformer layers whose token representations are highly redundant over time, exploiting the fact that hidden states often stagnate across consecutive middle layers.

In deep residual networks \( h_{l+1} = h_l + \text{Layer}(h_l) \), the output frequently changes infinitesimally between consecutive middle layers. We can quantify this informational stagnation via cosine similarity:

\[ \text{sim}(h_l, h_{l-1}) = \frac{h_l \cdot h_{l-1}}{\|h_l\| \, \|h_{l-1}\|} \]

If \( \text{sim} > \tau \) (where \( \tau \) is a high threshold like 0.99), layer \( l \) is contributing effectively zero new information. The network can bypass the dense Attention and MLP calculations, functionally setting \( h_{l+1} \approx h_{l-1} \).

sections.title: Static vs. Dynamic Pruning
sections.body:
• Static (Offline): Layers that are consistently redundant across a vast corpus are permanently sliced out of the model architecture.
  • Dynamic (TrimLLM / Adaptive Depth): Layer redundancy is evaluated on-the-fly per prompt. Different prompts trigger different layer bypasses, maintaining high capacity for complex reasoning while accelerating simple token generation.

This avoids the massive retraining costs of standard pruning/distillation workflows. By exploiting the inherent identity mechanisms of residual streams, practitioners cut inference latency purely mathematically post-hoc.

title: Test-Time Compute Scaling
summary: A paradigm asserting that allocating vast computational resources at inference time (via search, resampling, or iterative refinement) often yields higher accuracy gains on complex logic problems than indiscriminately scaling up pre-train…

papers.citation: 2024 · Snell et al. · Scaling LLM Test-Time Compute Optimally...

sections.body: A paradigm asserting that allocating vast computational resources at inference time (via search, resampling, or iterative refinement) often yields higher accuracy gains on complex logic problems than indiscriminately scaling up pre-training model parameters.

sections.title: Illustration

sections.title: Primary Mechanisms
sections.body:
• Best-of-N Sampling: Generate \( N \) stochastic answers and select the majority vote (self-consistency) or use a Process Reward Model. Plurality logic scales logarithmically: \( P_{\text{success}} = 1 - (1 - p)^N \).
